context:
Section 3 (Deterministic Voting Rule), discussion immediately after Theorem 2; page 8 of 21 in the provided text.

This might lead one to wonder whether one can obtain a tighter best-of-both-worlds guarantee with a utilitarian distortion of \(O(m^2)\) and a metric distortion of at most 3. We show that this is not possible and leave open the question of improving upon the metric distortion of \(9+\varepsilon\) in Theorem 1.
title: Improve metric distortion constant for deterministic best-of-both-worlds voting with full rankings
background_latex:
Let $N$ be a set of $n$ agents and $A$ a set of $m$ alternatives. Each agent $i\in N$ reports a strict ranking $\sigma_i\in L(A)$ over $A$, and the preference profile is $\vec\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n)\in L(A)^n$. A deterministic voting rule is a function $f:L(A)^n\to A$ that outputs a single alternative.

Metric framework. A (pseudo)metric is a function $d:(N\cup A)^2\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ satisfying $d(x,x)=0$, symmetry, and the triangle inequality. A profile $\vec\sigma$ is consistent with $d$ if for all agents $i$ and alternatives $X,Y\in A$, $X\succ_i Y$ implies $d(i,X)\le d(i,Y)$. The social cost of $X$ is $\mathrm{SC}(X,d)=\sum_{i\in N} d(i,X)$. The metric distortion of a deterministic rule $f$ on a profile $\vec\sigma$ is
\[\mathrm{dist}_M(f,\vec\sigma)=\sup_{d:\, d\ \text{consistent with}\ \vec\sigma}\ \frac{\mathrm{SC}(f(\vec\sigma),d)}{\min_{Y\in A} \mathrm{SC}(Y,d)}.\]

Utilitarian framework. Each agent $i$ has a unit-sum utility function $u_i:A\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ with $\sum_{X\in A} u_i(X)=1$. A profile $\vec\sigma$ is consistent with $\vec u=(u_1,\dots,u_n)$ if $X\succ_i Y$ implies $u_i(X)>u_i(Y)$. The social welfare of $X$ is $\mathrm{SW}(X,\vec u)=\sum_{i\in N} u_i(X)$. The utilitarian distortion of $f$ on $\vec\sigma$ is
\[\mathrm{dist}_U(f,\vec\sigma)=\sup_{\vec u:\, \vec u\ \text{consistent with}\ \vec\sigma}\ \frac{\max_{Y\in A} \mathrm{SW}(Y,\vec u)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(\vec\sigma),\vec u)}.\]

A deterministic rule is said to achieve a "best-of-both-worlds" guarantee (for full rankings) if it has metric distortion bounded by a constant independent of $m,n$ and utilitarian distortion on the order of $m^2$ (the asymptotically optimal deterministic order for unit-sum utilities). One known such rule, called Pruned Plurality Veto, achieves metric distortion $9+\varepsilon$ (for any fixed $\varepsilon>0$) while keeping utilitarian distortion $O(m^2/\varepsilon)$.
problem_definition_latex: Find a deterministic voting rule on full rankings whose utilitarian distortion is on the order of $m^2$ and whose metric distortion guarantee is strictly smaller than the currently known constant $9+\varepsilon$ (for fixed $\varepsilon>0$), i.e., improve the constant-factor upper bound on the worst-case metric distortion achievable simultaneously with an $\Theta(m^2)$-order utilitarian distortion guarantee.
latex:
Let $N$ be a set of $n$ agents and $A$ a set of $m$ alternatives. Each agent $i\in N$ reports a strict ranking $\sigma_i\in L(A)$ over $A$, and the preference profile is $\vec\sigma=(\sigma_1,\dots,\sigma_n)\in L(A)^n$. A deterministic voting rule is a function $f:L(A)^n\to A$ that outputs a single alternative.

Metric framework. A (pseudo)metric is a function $d:(N\cup A)^2\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ satisfying $d(x,x)=0$, symmetry, and the triangle inequality. A profile $\vec\sigma$ is consistent with $d$ if for all agents $i$ and alternatives $X,Y\in A$, $X\succ_i Y$ implies $d(i,X)\le d(i,Y)$. The social cost of $X$ is $\mathrm{SC}(X,d)=\sum_{i\in N} d(i,X)$. The metric distortion of a deterministic rule $f$ on a profile $\vec\sigma$ is
\[\mathrm{dist}_M(f,\vec\sigma)=\sup_{d:\, d\ \text{consistent with}\ \vec\sigma}\ \frac{\mathrm{SC}(f(\vec\sigma),d)}{\min_{Y\in A} \mathrm{SC}(Y,d)}.\]

Utilitarian framework. Each agent $i$ has a unit-sum utility function $u_i:A\to \mathbb{R}_{\ge 0}$ with $\sum_{X\in A} u_i(X)=1$. A profile $\vec\sigma$ is consistent with $\vec u=(u_1,\dots,u_n)$ if $X\succ_i Y$ implies $u_i(X)>u_i(Y)$. The social welfare of $X$ is $\mathrm{SW}(X,\vec u)=\sum_{i\in N} u_i(X)$. The utilitarian distortion of $f$ on $\vec\sigma$ is
\[\mathrm{dist}_U(f,\vec\sigma)=\sup_{\vec u:\, \vec u\ \text{consistent with}\ \vec\sigma}\ \frac{\max_{Y\in A} \mathrm{SW}(Y,\vec u)}{\mathrm{SW}(f(\vec\sigma),\vec u)}.\]

A deterministic rule is said to achieve a "best-of-both-worlds" guarantee (for full rankings) if it has metric distortion bounded by a constant independent of $m,n$ and utilitarian distortion on the order of $m^2$ (the asymptotically optimal deterministic order for unit-sum utilities). One known such rule, called Pruned Plurality Veto, achieves metric distortion $9+\varepsilon$ (for any fixed $\varepsilon>0$) while keeping utilitarian distortion $O(m^2/\varepsilon)$.

Find a deterministic voting rule on full rankings whose utilitarian distortion is on the order of $m^2$ and whose metric distortion guarantee is strictly smaller than the currently known constant $9+\varepsilon$ (for fixed $\varepsilon>0$), i.e., improve the constant-factor upper bound on the worst-case metric distortion achievable simultaneously with an $\Theta(m^2)$-order utilitarian distortion guarantee.
location_in_paper: Section 3 (Deterministic Voting Rule), discussion immediately after Theorem 2; page 8 of 21 in the provided text.
open_problem_quote: This might lead one to wonder whether one can obtain a tighter best-of-both-worlds guarantee with a utilitarian distortion of \(O(m^2)\) and a metric distortion of at most 3. We show that this is not possible and leave open the question of improving upon the metric distortion of \(9+\varepsilon\) in Theorem 1.
counterexample_suitability_rationale: One can computationally search over finite agent/alternative profiles and adversarially chosen metrics consistent with the rankings to test and compare candidate rules’ metric-distortion constants, but the space of deterministic rules is enormous and worst-case distortion involves a supremum over consistent metrics, which is nontrivial to optimize exactly.
alphaevolve_suitability_rationale: Candidate voting rules can be represented as programs and evaluated on large suites of generated preference profiles with metric-adversary subroutines (e.g., LP/MIP formulations for worst-case consistent metrics in restricted families) to obtain a computable fitness proxy; however, faithful evaluation of the true worst-case metric distortion is expensive and may require careful relaxation or restriction to informative metric families.

verification.notice: Extracted from the source paper and passed an automated fidelity audit; not independently verified as still open.

literature_review.synthesis: No relevant Scholar-organic forward citations were available for this problem. The forward-citation review therefore cannot identify follow-up work that resolves or materially advances the problem.
